items.title: The five components and diagnostic criteria (NCEP ATP III)
items.content:
Metabolic syndrome (syndrome X, insulin resistance syndrome) is a cluster of metabolic abnormalities that raise the risk of cardiovascular disease AND type 2 diabetes. By NCEP:ATP III, diagnosis requires 3 or more of:
• Central obesity — waist >102 cm (men), >88 cm (women)
• Triglycerides ≥150 mg/dL (or on treatment)
• HDL-C <40 mg/dL (men) / <50 mg/dL (women) (or on treatment)
• Blood pressure ≥130 systolic or ≥85 diastolic (or on treatment)
• Fasting glucose ≥100 mg/dL (or on treatment / known type 2 DM)

Note each component can be met by being on drug treatment for it. Waist-circumference thresholds are adjusted by ethnicity.
keyPoints: Need ≥3 of 5: central obesity, ↑TG, ↓HDL, ↑BP, ↑fasting glucose.
Being on treatment for a component counts as meeting it.
Predicts both CVD and type 2 diabetes.
items.reference: Definition/Table 420-1: "Three or more of the following: Central obesity: waist circumference >102 cm (males), >88 cm (females); Hypertriglyceridemia ≥150 mg/dL... Low HDL <40/<50... Hypertension ≥130/≥85... Fasting plasma glucose ≥100 mg/dL."

items.title: Why insulin resistance is the unifying mechanism
items.content:
The most accepted unifying hypothesis is insulin resistance. Its natural history begins with POSTPRANDIAL hyperinsulinemia, then FASTING hyperinsulinemia, and finally hyperglycemia as compensation fails.

A key driver is excess circulating free fatty acids (FFAs) from adipose lipolysis. Crucially, inhibition of lipolysis is the MOST insulin-sensitive action of insulin — so even mild insulin resistance disinhibits lipolysis, releasing more FFAs, which further blunt insulin action (a vicious cycle). Excess FFAs impair insulin-mediated glucose uptake in muscle and increase hepatic glucose and triglyceride production. This single mechanism links the obesity, dyslipidemia, glucose intolerance, and (partly) hypertension of the syndrome.
keyPoints: Sequence: postprandial → fasting hyperinsulinemia → hyperglycemia.
Anti-lipolysis is the most insulin-sensitive pathway → early FFA excess.
FFAs impair muscle glucose uptake and raise hepatic glucose/TG output.
items.reference: Etiology: "The most accepted and unifying hypothesis to describe the pathophysiology of metabolic syndrome is insulin resistance... the inhibition of lipolysis in adipose tissue is the most sensitive pathway of insulin action."

items.title: Why visceral fat matters more than subcutaneous fat
items.content:
Waist circumference is the hardest component to define because it doesn't distinguish subcutaneous (SC) from visceral fat. The distinction is physiologically critical:
• VISCERAL fat drains into the PORTAL circulation, delivering free fatty acids directly to the liver → more hepatic glucose/VLDL production and insulin resistance.
• Abdominal SC fat releases lipolysis products into the SYSTEMIC circulation, with fewer direct hepatic effects.

This explains why, for the same waist circumference, risk differs by population: Asians/Asian Indians carry relatively more visceral fat (higher metabolic-syndrome risk at lower waist), whereas African Americans have predominantly SC fat — hence ethnicity-specific waist thresholds. It also explains the lean, insulin-resistant ('normal-weight') phenotype.
keyPoints: Visceral fat → portal FFA delivery to liver → more metabolic harm.
SC fat drains systemically (less hepatic effect).
Ethnicity-specific waist cutoffs; normal-weight people can be insulin-resistant.
items.reference: Increased Waist Circumference: "With increases in visceral adipose tissue, adipose tissue-derived free fatty acids reach the liver more readily... increases in abdominal SC fat release lipolysis products into the systemic circulation and therefore have fewer direct effects on hepatic metabolism."

items.title: Why metabolic syndrome produces high TG, low HDL, and small dense LDL
items.content:
The lipid triad of metabolic syndrome flows from FFA-driven hepatic VLDL overproduction:
• High triglycerides — increased FFA flux to the liver raises apoB-containing VLDL output; elevated apoC-III (which inhibits LPL) further slows TG-rich remnant clearance.
• Low HDL-C — in the presence of high TG, CETP swaps triglyceride into HDL for cholesteryl ester, producing small, dense HDL that is cleared faster, lowering HDL-C.
• Small dense LDL — once fasting TG exceeds ~180 mg/dL, LDL particles become small and dense (more atherogenic), with increased LDL particle number/apoB.

So a single insulin-resistant, high-FFA state generates all three changes; hypertriglyceridemia is an excellent marker of the insulin-resistant condition.
keyPoints: FFA → hepatic VLDL overproduction → high TG (apoC-III worsens it).
High TG + CETP → small dense HDL (cleared faster) → low HDL-C.
High TG → small dense LDL with increased particle number/apoB.
items.reference: Dyslipidemia: "free fatty acid flux from adipose tissue to the liver results in increased production of apoB-containing, triglyceride-rich VLDLs... a decrease in the cholesterol content of HDL... make the HDL particle small and dense... a predominance of small dense LDLs."

items.title: Why insulin resistance contributes to hypertension
items.content:
Normally insulin causes nitric-oxide-mediated vasoDILATION while promoting renal sodium reabsorption. In insulin resistance the VASODILATORY effect is lost but the sodium-retaining effect is PRESERVED — and insulin's sympathetic-nervous-system activation also persists. The net result favors hypertension (sodium retention + sympathetic drive + impaired vasodilation).

Additional contributors: increased adipose angiotensinogen → more angiotensin II/vasoconstriction; perivascular-adipose reactive oxygen species impairing endothelial function; and hyperuricemia (which impairs macula-densa nitric oxide synthase and stimulates the renin-angiotensin system). Still, insulin resistance explains only part of the hypertension in the syndrome.
keyPoints: Insulin resistance: lose vasodilation, keep sodium retention + sympathetic activation.
Adipose angiotensinogen and perivascular ROS add to hypertension.
Hyperuricemia also raises BP (impairs NO, activates RAAS).
items.reference: Hypertension: "in the setting of insulin resistance, the vasodilatory effect of insulin is lost but the renal effect on sodium reabsorption is preserved. Insulin also increases the activity of the sympathetic nervous system."

items.title: Why adipose tissue behaves like an inflammatory endocrine organ
items.content:
Expanded adipose tissue is not inert — it secretes hormones and cytokines that drive the syndrome:
• Pro-disease: increased free fatty acids; proinflammatory cytokines (IL-1, IL-6, IL-18, TNF-α, resistin) largely from adipose-tissue macrophages, raising systemic CRP; and PAI-1/visfatin/fetuin-A/asprosin that worsen insulin resistance.
• Protective: ADIPONECTIN, an anti-inflammatory, insulin-sensitizing adipokine that suppresses hepatic gluconeogenesis and increases muscle glucose uptake/fatty-acid oxidation (via AMP kinase) — its levels FALL in metabolic syndrome.
• Leptin: physiologically reduces appetite and improves insulin sensitivity, but obesity causes leptin RESISTANCE.

Thus obesity tips the adipokine balance toward inflammation and insulin resistance.
keyPoints: Adipose macrophages release IL-6, TNF-α, etc. → systemic inflammation (↑CRP).
Adiponectin (insulin-sensitizing, anti-inflammatory) is reduced.
Obesity → leptin resistance.
items.reference: Adiponectin/Cytokines: "Adiponectin is an anti-inflammatory cytokine produced exclusively by adipocytes. Adiponectin enhances insulin sensitivity... Reductions in adiponectin levels are common in metabolic syndrome."

items.title: Why metabolic syndrome predicts both diabetes and cardiovascular disease
items.content:
The syndrome's prognostic value is twofold:
• DIABETES — its greatest predictive value: ~75% of people with type 2 DM or impaired glucose tolerance have metabolic syndrome, and the syndrome raises type 2 DM risk 3–5 fold (Framingham population-attributable risk ~62% men/47% women) — though elevated fasting glucose explains most of this.
• CARDIOVASCULAR — people with metabolic syndrome are ~2× as likely to die of CVD and have ~3× the risk of MI or stroke; up to ~60% of CHD patients >75 have it.

Clinically, identifying the syndrome flags a patient for aggressive multifactorial risk reduction (weight, lipids, BP, glucose), even before overt diabetes appears.
keyPoints: ~75% of type 2 DM/IGT patients have metabolic syndrome; 3–5× diabetes risk.
~2× CVD mortality, ~3× MI/stroke risk.
Fasting glucose drives most of the diabetes prediction.
items.reference: Risk: "The great majority (~75%) of patients with type 2 diabetes or impaired glucose tolerance have metabolic syndrome... Individuals with metabolic syndrome are twice as likely to die of CVD as those who do not, and their risk of acute myocardial infarction or stroke is threefold higher."

items.title: Why metabolic syndrome causes fatty liver (MAFLD/MASH)
items.content:
Metabolic dysfunction-associated fatty liver disease (MAFLD) is now the most common liver disease and is largely a consequence of the insulin resistance of metabolic syndrome. Mechanism: increased free-fatty-acid flux to the liver plus reduced intrahepatic fatty-acid oxidation → increased triglyceride synthesis and hepatocellular fat accumulation, with variable inflammation/oxidative stress.

It affects up to ~65% of patients with metabolic syndrome; over half of those have the more serious MASH (steatohepatitis), a precursor of cirrhosis, end-stage liver disease, and hepatocellular carcinoma. MAFLD is also independently associated with cardiovascular (coronary) disease. Assess with the FIB-4 fibrosis score or elastography.
keyPoints: Insulin resistance → ↑FFA + ↓oxidation → hepatic steatosis.
Up to ~65% of metabolic syndrome patients; half have MASH (→cirrhosis/HCC).
Independently linked to coronary disease; stage with FIB-4/elastography.
items.reference: MAFLD: "MAFLD has become the most common liver disease, in part a consequence of the insulin resistance of metabolic syndrome... MAFLD affects ~10% of the nonobese population and up to 65% of patients with metabolic syndrome."

items.title: Why obstructive sleep apnea is tied to metabolic syndrome
items.content:
Obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) clusters with obesity, hypertension, proinflammatory cytokines, impaired glucose tolerance, and insulin resistance. Strikingly, OSA may PREDICT metabolic syndrome even in the ABSENCE of excess adiposity, and when compared with weight-matched controls, insulin resistance is MORE severe in those with apnea — suggesting OSA itself contributes to insulin resistance.

Clinically: ask every metabolic-syndrome patient about OSA symptoms and order a sleep study if present. CPAP treatment improves insulin sensitivity — making OSA a modifiable contributor worth identifying.
keyPoints: OSA can predict metabolic syndrome even without obesity.
Insulin resistance is worse in OSA than in weight-matched controls.
Screen all patients for OSA; CPAP improves insulin sensitivity.
items.reference: Obstructive Sleep Apnea: "obstructive sleep apnea may predict metabolic syndrome, even in the absence of excess adiposity... Continuous positive airway pressure treatment improves insulin sensitivity in patients with obstructive sleep apnea."

items.title: Why weight loss is the primary treatment
items.content:
Central obesity is the driving force, so weight reduction is the cornerstone. As little as 5% (and more so 10%) weight loss improves insulin sensitivity and favorably modifies multiple components (TG, HDL, glucose, BP) at once — a single intervention addressing the whole cluster.

Practical principles: caloric restriction is the MOST important element for losing weight, while increased physical activity is most important for MAINTAINING the loss; ~3500 kcal ≈ 1 lb of fat, so a ~500-kcal daily deficit ≈ 1 lb/week. Adherence matters more than the specific diet (low-carb gives faster initial loss but similar results at 1 year); a high-quality dietary pattern (fruit, vegetables, whole grains, lean protein/fish) is encouraged, with caution about saturated-fat-rich low-carb diets in ASCVD-risk patients.
keyPoints: 5–10% weight loss improves insulin sensitivity and multiple components.
Caloric restriction drives loss; activity maintains it.
Adherence > diet type; favor a high-quality dietary pattern.
items.reference: Treatment — Lifestyle: "weight reduction is the primary approach... With at least 5% and more so with 10% weight reduction, improvement in insulin sensitivity results in favorable modifications in many components... Caloric restriction is the most important component, whereas increases in physical activity are important for maintenance."

items.title: Pharmacologic weight loss in metabolic syndrome
items.content:
When lifestyle is insufficient, weight-loss drugs fall into appetite suppressants and absorption inhibitors:
• Phentermine (short-term only); phentermine/topiramate ER (~8% loss; paresthesias, insomnia); naltrexone/bupropion (avoid in seizure disorders and uncontrolled hypertension — raises pulse/BP).
• GLP-1 receptor agonists: high-dose liraglutide 3.0 mg (~6%) and semaglutide 2.4 mg weekly (~15% over 68 weeks); tirzepatide (dual GIP/GLP-1) gives dose-dependent loss up to ~21%.
• Orlistat inhibits ~30% of fat absorption (~4% extra loss; reduces incident diabetes, especially in IGT) — oily fecal leakage limits use.

Greater weight reduction yields greater improvement in metabolic-syndrome components, including prevention of progression from prediabetes to diabetes.
keyPoints: Semaglutide 2.4 mg ~15%, tirzepatide up to ~21%; liraglutide 3.0 mg ~6%.
Naltrexone/bupropion contraindicated in seizures/uncontrolled HTN.
Orlistat (~4%) reduces incident diabetes; causes oily leakage.
items.reference: Treatment — Obesity: "Semaglutide (2.4 mg weekly) has been shown to produce an average weight loss of 14.9% over 68 weeks. Tirzepatide... reduction in weight ranging from -15.0%... to 20.9%... Orlistat inhibits fat absorption by ~30%."

items.title: Why ACE inhibitors/ARBs are first-line for blood pressure here
items.content:
In metabolic syndrome WITHOUT diabetes, the preferred initial antihypertensive is an ACE inhibitor or an angiotensin-receptor blocker, because they are effective and well tolerated and act on the renin-angiotensin system that is upregulated in the insulin-resistant/obese state (increased adipose angiotensinogen → angiotensin II). Additional agents: diuretic, calcium channel blocker, beta-blocker, and mineralocorticoid-receptor antagonists (including finerenone).

A sodium-restricted, fruit/vegetable/whole-grain/low-fat-dairy (DASH-type) dietary pattern is advocated for all, and home BP monitoring helps maintain control.
keyPoints: First-line BP drug in (non-diabetic) metabolic syndrome: ACE-I or ARB.
Add diuretic, CCB, beta-blocker, or MR antagonist (e.g., finerenone) as needed.
DASH-type sodium-restricted diet + home BP monitoring.
items.reference: Treatment — Blood Pressure: "In patients who have metabolic syndrome without diabetes, the best choice for the initial antihypertensive medication is an angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor or an angiotensin II receptor blocker."

items.title: Why pioglitazone and metformin help metabolic syndrome
items.content:
Because insulin resistance is the core defect, insulin-sensitizing drugs reduce the syndrome's burden:
• Metformin and TZDs both enhance hepatic insulin action and suppress endogenous glucose production; TZDs (but not metformin) ALSO improve insulin-mediated glucose uptake in muscle and fat.
• In a meta-analysis, pioglitazone (a TZD) reduced ASCVD events in patients with insulin resistance, prediabetes, and type 2 DM — but at the cost of weight gain, fractures, and heart failure/edema.
• TZDs benefit MAFLD; metformin benefits PCOS; both reduce inflammatory markers; GLP-1RAs improve insulin sensitivity in proportion to weight loss.

For impaired fasting glucose, lifestyle reduces incident diabetes more than metformin does.
keyPoints: Metformin/TZDs improve insulin sensitivity (TZDs also act on muscle/fat).
Pioglitazone reduced ASCVD events but causes weight gain/fractures/CHF.
Lifestyle prevents diabetes better than metformin in impaired fasting glucose.
items.reference: Treatment — Insulin Resistance: "the TZD pioglitazone versus placebo was associated with reduction in ASCVD events in patients with insulin resistance (metabolic syndrome), prediabetes, and type 2 diabetes. However, adverse effects including weight gain, bone fracture, and congestive heart failure... were seen."

items.title: Why triglyceride-lowering is mainly for pancreatitis, not proven CVD benefit
items.content:
Although TG >150 is a defining component, treating triglycerides has NOT been proven to independently reduce ASCVD. Guidelines treat fasting TG >500 mg/dL primarily to prevent pancreatitis. Post-hoc fibrate analyses did not show a TG-related reduction in primary ASCVD outcomes, and the degree of TG lowering did not predict benefit.

Nuances: fibrates lower TG ~30–45% (fenofibrate preferred with statins to limit CYP3A4-mediated myopathy risk); the VA-HIT gemfibrozil benefit was concentrated in hyperinsulinemic/diabetic (metabolic-syndrome) men. For omega-3s, the EPA-ONLY trials (JELIS, REDUCE-IT) reduced ASCVD, whereas EPA+DHA (STRENGTH) was futile. Niacin lowers TG ~20–35% but raises glucose in diabetics and has not reduced events.
keyPoints: Treat TG >500 to prevent pancreatitis; TG-lowering not proven to cut ASCVD.
Fenofibrate > gemfibrozil when combined with a statin (less myopathy).
EPA-only omega-3 (JELIS/REDUCE-IT) helped; EPA+DHA (STRENGTH) did not.
items.reference: Treatment — Triglycerides: "fasting triglycerides >500 mg/dL should be treated to prevent more serious hypertriglyceridemia and pancreatitis... lowering triglycerides with any of the pharmaceuticals has not been proven to be an independent predictor of CVD outcomes."

items.title: Why raising HDL-C is not a goal in metabolic syndrome
items.content:
Few drugs raise HDL-C: statins, fibrates, and bile acid sequestrants have modest (5–10%) effects; ezetimibe and omega-3s have none; niacin is the only reliable HDL-raiser (up to ~30%). But after multiple trials of niacin added to statins, there is NO evidence that raising HDL-C with niacin reduces ASCVD events.

This mirrors the broader lesson (Chap. 419) that low HDL-C is a risk marker reflecting the underlying insulin-resistant state, not a causal target. So management focuses on weight loss and lowering apoB/LDL-C rather than pharmacologically raising HDL-C.
keyPoints: Niacin raises HDL up to ~30% but does not reduce events.
Low HDL is a marker of insulin resistance, not a treatment target.
Focus on weight loss and LDL-C/apoB lowering.
items.reference: Treatment — HDL: "Nicotinic acid is the only currently available drug with predictable HDL cholesterol-raising properties... there is no evidence that raising HDL cholesterol with nicotinic acid beneficially affects ASCVD events."

items.title: Statin-first LDL strategy in metabolic syndrome
items.content:
Metabolic-syndrome criteria were created to go BEYOND LDL-C, assuming LDL goals were already addressed. Still, LDL-lowering remains central: statins are first-line (LDL-C reduction 15–60%; doubling the dose adds only ~6%). Specific triggers: LDL-C ≥190 → high-intensity statin; type 2 DM aged 40–75 → at least a moderate-intensity statin (high-intensity if risk is high); non-diabetic metabolic syndrome → use the 10-year ASCVD risk estimator (≥7.5% → discuss statin; a coronary calcium score can help borderline decisions).

Add-ons in order: ezetimibe (15–20%), bempedoic acid (±ezetimibe up to 35%; raises uric acid), PCSK9 inhibitors (45–60%; rarely needed unless FH or inadequate statin response). Bile acid sequestrants can raise TG — use cautiously if fasting TG >300.
keyPoints: Statin first; LDL ≥190 → high-intensity; T2DM 40–75 → ≥moderate.
Non-diabetic: 10-yr ASCVD risk ≥7.5% → discuss statin; CAC helps borderline cases.
Add ezetimibe → bempedoic acid → PCSK9i; avoid bile acid resins if TG >300.
items.reference: Treatment — LDL: "treatment with statins, which lower LDL cholesterol by 15–60%, is the first-choice medication intervention... patients with a risk ≥7.5%... should have a discussion with their provider about initiating statin therapy."

items.title: How common metabolic syndrome is, and how it varies
items.content:
Prevalence in U.S. adults is ~33–35% and rises sharply with age — from ~19.5% at 20–39 years to ~48.6% at ≥60 (nearly half of those over 60), with women more often affected after 60. Childhood obesity is pushing the syndrome into younger ages (~12% of obese and ~30% of overweight children show features).

Population differences are real: abdominal obesity is similarly prevalent across U.S. races, but the other components vary; recent U.S. trends show rising hyperglycemia, stable central obesity/low-HDL/hypertension, and DECLINING high-triglyceride prevalence. Heritability of the individual metabolic traits exceeds 50%, though no single gene defines the syndrome.
keyPoints: ~1/3 of U.S. adults; ~50% of those >60.
Increasingly seen in obese/overweight children.
Heritability of each trait >50%; no single causal gene.
items.reference: Epidemiology: "The prevalence of metabolic syndrome... is ~35%... from 19.5% among those aged 20–39 years to 48.6% among those aged ≥60 years... heritability estimates for each of the metabolic traits exceed 50%."

items.title: Why metabolic syndrome is usually silent on exam
items.content:
Metabolic syndrome typically causes NO symptoms. On exam, elevated waist circumference and blood pressure are the usual (and often only) findings — and finding either should prompt a search for the other biochemical components.

Less commonly, signs of SEVERE insulin resistance appear: acanthosis nigricans (velvety hyperpigmented skin) or lipoatrophy. Because these reflect marked insulin resistance, the routine components (high TG, low HDL, high glucose/BP) are far more common presentations. The clinical message: actively measure waist, BP, fasting lipids, and glucose rather than waiting for symptoms.
keyPoints: Asymptomatic; elevated waist and BP are the main exam findings.
Acanthosis nigricans/lipoatrophy signal severe insulin resistance (less common).
Diagnosis is by active measurement, not symptoms.
items.reference: Clinical Features: "Metabolic syndrome typically is not associated with symptoms... waist circumference and blood pressure are often elevated... Much less frequently, lipoatrophy or acanthosis nigricans is present."

items.title: How to work up a patient for metabolic syndrome
items.content:
Diagnosis is by the Table 420-1 criteria using bedside + lab data. Practical workup:
• History: ask ALL patients about obstructive sleep apnea symptoms and premenopausal women about PCOS; take a family history for CVD/diabetes.
• Bedside: blood pressure and waist circumference.
• Core labs: fasting lipids and glucose.
• Individualized labs (markers of insulin resistance/associated conditions): apoB, hsCRP, fibrinogen, uric acid, urine albumin/creatinine ratio, liver function.
• Targeted tests: sleep study if OSA symptoms; testosterone/LH/FSH if PCOS suspected; FIB-4 score or elastography to assess MAFLD.
keyPoints: Screen all for OSA; premenopausal women for PCOS.
Core labs: fasting lipids + glucose; plus BP and waist.
Add hsCRP/apoB/uric acid/urine ACR/LFTs and FIB-4 as indicated.
items.reference: Diagnosis: "The medical history should include evaluation of symptoms for obstructive sleep apnea in all patients and polycystic ovary syndrome in premenopausal women... Measurement of fasting lipids and glucose is needed."

items.title: Why PCOS overlaps with metabolic syndrome
items.content:
Polycystic ovary syndrome is highly associated with insulin resistance (in 50–80% of patients) and with metabolic syndrome (prevalence 12–60% depending on phenotype). The shared substrate is insulin resistance, which both drives the metabolic cluster and contributes to the ovarian/androgen abnormalities of PCOS.

Clinically: in a premenopausal woman with metabolic-syndrome features, screen for PCOS (anovulation, hyperandrogenism) and, if suspected, measure testosterone, LH, and FSH. Metformin (an insulin sensitizer) has benefit in PCOS, reinforcing insulin resistance as the common link.
keyPoints: PCOS: insulin resistance in 50–80%; strong overlap with metabolic syndrome.
Screen premenopausal women; check testosterone/LH/FSH if suspected.
Metformin helps PCOS (shared insulin-resistance mechanism).
items.reference: Polycystic Ovary Syndrome: "Polycystic ovary syndrome is highly associated with insulin resistance (50–80%) and metabolic syndrome, with a prevalence of the syndrome between 12 and 60%."

items.title: Metabolic surgery for metabolic syndrome
items.content:
Metabolic (bariatric) surgery is an option when lifestyle and drugs are insufficient: indicated at BMI >40, or >35 with comorbidities — and increasingly at BMI as low as 30 with type 2 diabetes. Gastric bypass or vertical sleeve gastrectomy produces dramatic weight loss and improvement in most features of metabolic syndrome, and a survival benefit with gastric bypass has been demonstrated.

This reflects the principle that aggressively reducing adiposity reverses the underlying insulin resistance and thereby the whole cluster.
keyPoints: Indicated at BMI >40, or >35 with comorbidities; consider at ≥30 with T2DM.
Gastric bypass/sleeve → major weight loss + improvement in most components.
Gastric bypass has shown a survival benefit.
items.reference: Treatment — Obesity: "Metabolic or bariatric surgery is an important option for patients with metabolic syndrome who have a BMI >40 kg/m² or >35 kg/m² with comorbidities... A survival benefit with gastric bypass has also been realized."

items.title: Why sedentary lifestyle independently raises risk
items.content:
Physical inactivity and low cardiorespiratory fitness independently predict cardiovascular events and death, and many components of the syndrome track with sedentary behavior — increased (central) adiposity, lower HDL, higher triglycerides, blood pressure, and glucose in susceptible people.

A concrete marker: compared with <1 h/day of screen time (TV/video/computer), >4 h/day doubles the risk of metabolic syndrome. This is why increasing physical activity (and reducing sedentary time) is a core lifestyle target, both for weight-loss maintenance and for independent cardiometabolic benefit.
keyPoints: Low fitness/inactivity independently predicts CVD events and death.
>4 h/day screen time ≈ double the metabolic-syndrome risk.
Reducing sedentary time is a core target, beyond weight loss.
items.reference: Sedentary Lifestyle: "those who do so for >4 h daily have a twofold increased risk of the metabolic syndrome."

items.title: How much activity to prescribe (and screening first)
items.content:
Before prescribing exercise, ensure it doesn't incur cardiac risk — some high-risk patients need formal cardiovascular evaluation first, and inactive patients should increase activity gradually to enhance adherence and avoid injury.

Dosing: 60–90 minutes of moderate-to-high-intensity activity daily is required to achieve weight loss, but even 30 minutes of moderate-intensity activity daily confers health benefit if the higher amount isn't feasible. Importantly, routine activities (gardening, walking, housecleaning) count — physical activity should not be defined solely as formal exercise.
keyPoints: Risk-assess high-risk patients before an exercise program; progress gradually.
60–90 min/day for weight loss; ≥30 min/day for health benefit.
Routine activities (walking, gardening) count toward the goal.
items.reference: Treatment — Physical Activity: "60–90 min of moderate- to high-intensity daily activity is required to achieve this goal... a health benefit will follow from at least 30 min of moderate-intensity activity daily."

items.title: Why hyperuricemia accompanies metabolic syndrome
items.content:
Hyperuricemia is a consequence of insulin resistance: insulin enhances renal tubular reabsorption of uric acid, so the hyperinsulinemic, insulin-resistant state raises serum urate. Beyond being a marker, uric acid may CONTRIBUTE to hypertension — it impairs nitric oxide synthase in the macula densa of the kidney and stimulates the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system; reducing uric acid has normalized blood pressure in hyperuricemic adolescents with hypertension.

Related endothelial markers (elevated asymmetric dimethylarginine, an NO-synthase inhibitor, and increased urine albumin/creatinine ratio) also reflect the insulin-resistant endothelial state.
keyPoints: Insulin ↑ renal uric-acid reabsorption → hyperuricemia.
Uric acid may raise BP (impairs NO, activates RAAS).
Microalbuminuria/ADMA reflect endothelial dysfunction.
items.reference: Hyperuricemia: "Hyperuricemia reflects defects in insulin action on the renal tubular reabsorption of uric acid and may contribute to hypertension through its effect on the endothelium."

items.title: Why lifestyle beats metformin for preventing diabetes
items.content:
In people with impaired fasting glucose who do not yet have diabetes, a lifestyle intervention (weight reduction, dietary saturated-fat restriction, increased physical activity) reduces the incidence of type 2 diabetes. Metformin also reduces incident diabetes, but its effect is LESS pronounced than that of lifestyle intervention.

Clinical implication: lifestyle change is the first-line strategy for diabetes prevention in metabolic syndrome/prediabetes, with metformin as an adjunct (e.g., higher-risk individuals). In patients who already have type 2 DM, aggressive glycemic control can also favorably modify triglycerides and HDL-C.
keyPoints: Lifestyle reduces incident diabetes more than metformin in IFG.
Metformin is an effective adjunct for higher-risk prediabetes.
In established T2DM, good glycemic control improves TG/HDL.
items.reference: Impaired Fasting Glucose: "a lifestyle intervention that includes weight reduction, dietary saturated fat restriction, and increased physical activity has been shown to reduce the incidence of type 2 diabetes. Metformin also reduces the incidence of diabetes, although the effect is less pronounced than that of lifestyle intervention."

items.title: Why lipodystrophy causes severe metabolic syndrome
items.content:
Lipodystrophic disorders — genetic (Berardinelli-Seip congenital generalized lipodystrophy, Dunnigan familial partial lipodystrophy) and acquired (HIV-related, including with certain antiretrovirals) — commonly present with metabolic syndrome. The loss of functional adipose storage capacity forces lipid into ectopic sites (liver, muscle), producing severe insulin resistance and many components of the syndrome.

This illustrates the principle that it is not simply the amount of fat but the FAILURE of healthy adipose storage (and resulting ectopic fat) that drives insulin resistance — the same reason lean, fat-redistributed patients can be profoundly insulin-resistant.
keyPoints: Genetic and HIV-related lipodystrophy → severe insulin resistance.
Loss of adipose storage → ectopic fat (liver/muscle) → metabolic syndrome.
Shows that fat dysfunction, not just fat mass, drives the syndrome.
items.reference: Lipodystrophy: "Both genetic lipodystrophy (e.g., Berardinelli-Seip congenital lipodystrophy, Dunnigan familial partial lipodystrophy) and acquired lipodystrophy (e.g., HIV-related lipodystrophy...) may give rise to severe insulin resistance and many of the components of metabolic syndrome."

items.question: By NCEP:ATP III criteria, how many of the five components are required to diagnose metabolic syndrome?
items.explanation: Metabolic syndrome (NCEP:ATP III) requires 3 or more of: central obesity, triglycerides ≥150, low HDL-C, blood pressure ≥130/85, and fasting glucose ≥100 mg/dL (with drug treatment for a component counting as meeting it).
items.reference: Table 420-1: "Three or more of the following: Central obesity... Hypertriglyceridemia ≥150 mg/dL... Low HDL... Hypertension ≥130/≥85... Fasting plasma glucose ≥100 mg/dL."

items.question: Which HDL-C and blood pressure thresholds define components of the metabolic syndrome?
items.explanation: The HDL-C cutoffs are <40 mg/dL in men and <50 mg/dL in women, and the blood pressure threshold is ≥130 systolic or ≥85 diastolic (or being on treatment). Note these BP cutoffs are lower than the usual hypertension threshold.
items.reference: Table 420-1: "Low HDL cholesterol: <40 mg/dL and <50 mg/dL for men and women... Hypertension: blood pressure ≥130 mmHg systolic or ≥85 mmHg diastolic."

items.question: Which is considered the central, unifying pathophysiologic mechanism of the metabolic syndrome?
items.explanation: Insulin resistance is the most accepted unifying hypothesis. It is heralded by postprandial then fasting hyperinsulinemia and ultimately hyperglycemia, and links the obesity, dyslipidemia, glucose intolerance, and (partly) hypertension of the syndrome.
items.reference: Etiology: "The most accepted and unifying hypothesis to describe the pathophysiology of metabolic syndrome is insulin resistance."

items.question: Why does visceral (intra-abdominal) fat have a greater metabolic impact than subcutaneous fat?
options: Its venous drainage is portal, delivering free fatty acids directly to the liver
items.explanation: Visceral fat drains into the portal circulation, so its free fatty acids reach the liver directly, increasing hepatic glucose and VLDL production and insulin resistance. Subcutaneous fat drains systemically with fewer direct hepatic effects — explaining ethnic differences in risk at the same waist.
items.reference: Increased Waist Circumference: "With increases in visceral adipose tissue, adipose tissue-derived free fatty acids reach the liver more readily."

items.question: A lean South Asian man with a normal BMI has hypertriglyceridemia, low HDL, hypertension, and impaired fasting glucose. What does this illustrate?
options: Metabolic syndrome cannot occur in normal-weight people
Normal-weight individuals can be insulin-resistant and have metabolic syndrome, partly due to relatively greater visceral fat
items.explanation: Normal-weight individuals can be insulin-resistant and meet metabolic-syndrome criteria; this 'normal-weight' phenotype is especially common in India, Southeast Asia, and Central America, reflecting relatively greater visceral adiposity at a given BMI/waist — hence ethnicity-specific waist thresholds.
items.reference: Risk Factors: "despite the importance of obesity, patients who are of normal weight may also be insulin-resistant and may have metabolic syndrome. This phenotype is particularly evident for populations in India, Southeast Asia, and Central America."

items.question: Which lipid profile is characteristic of the metabolic syndrome?
options: Low triglycerides, high HDL, large buoyant LDL
High triglycerides, low HDL, and small dense LDL
Isolated high LDL with normal triglycerides
items.explanation: Free-fatty-acid-driven hepatic VLDL overproduction (worsened by apoC-III inhibiting LPL) raises triglycerides; CETP-mediated remodeling then produces small dense HDL (low HDL-C) and, when TG is high, small dense (more atherogenic) LDL with increased particle number/apoB.
items.reference: Dyslipidemia: "increased production of apoB-containing, triglyceride-rich VLDLs... make the HDL particle small and dense... a predominance of small dense LDLs."

items.question: Approximately how much does metabolic syndrome increase the risk of developing type 2 diabetes?
items.explanation: Metabolic syndrome raises the risk of type 2 diabetes about 3- to 5-fold, and ~75% of people with type 2 DM or impaired glucose tolerance have the syndrome. Elevated fasting glucose accounts for most of this predictive power.
items.reference: Type 2 Diabetes: "the risk for type 2 diabetes among patients with metabolic syndrome is increased three- to fivefold."

items.question: Compared with people without it, individuals with metabolic syndrome have approximately what cardiovascular risk?
options: About 2× CVD mortality and 3× the risk of myocardial infarction or stroke
items.explanation: People with metabolic syndrome are about twice as likely to die of cardiovascular disease and have roughly threefold higher risk of acute MI or stroke. Up to ~60% of CHD patients over 75 have the syndrome.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Disease: "Individuals with metabolic syndrome are twice as likely to die of CVD as those who do not, and their risk of acute myocardial infarction or stroke is threefold higher."

items.question: What proportion of patients with metabolic syndrome have metabolic-associated fatty liver disease (MAFLD)?
options: MAFLD is unrelated to metabolic syndrome
items.explanation: MAFLD affects up to ~65% of patients with metabolic syndrome (and ~10% of the nonobese population). Over half of MAFLD patients have the more serious MASH, a precursor of cirrhosis, end-stage liver disease, and hepatocellular carcinoma; MAFLD is also independently linked to coronary disease.
items.reference: MAFLD: "MAFLD affects ~10% of the nonobese population and up to 65% of patients with metabolic syndrome; over half of these patients have metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH)."

items.question: Which statement about obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) and metabolic syndrome is correct?
options: OSA only occurs in obese patients and never relates to insulin resistance
OSA may predict metabolic syndrome even without excess adiposity, and CPAP improves insulin sensitivity
OSA is unrelated to insulin resistance
items.explanation: OSA is associated with insulin resistance and may predict metabolic syndrome even in the absence of excess adiposity; insulin resistance is more severe than in weight-matched controls, and CPAP improves insulin sensitivity. All metabolic-syndrome patients should be screened for OSA symptoms.
items.reference: Obstructive Sleep Apnea: "obstructive sleep apnea may predict metabolic syndrome, even in the absence of excess adiposity... Continuous positive airway pressure treatment improves insulin sensitivity."

items.question: What is the primary, first-line treatment of the metabolic syndrome?
items.explanation: Because central obesity is the driving force, weight reduction (caloric restriction, increased activity, behavior modification) is the primary approach; even 5–10% weight loss improves insulin sensitivity and multiple components simultaneously. Drug therapy targets individual components when needed.
items.reference: Treatment — Lifestyle: "weight reduction is the primary approach to the disorder. With at least 5% and more so with 10% weight reduction, improvement in insulin sensitivity results in favorable modifications in many components."

items.question: Roughly how much daily caloric deficit produces about 1 pound (0.45 kg) of weight loss per week?
options: Caloric deficit does not affect weight
items.explanation: Since ~3500 kcal corresponds to ~1 lb of adipose tissue, a daily deficit of ~500 kcal yields about 1 lb/week of weight loss. Adherence to the diet matters more than the specific diet composition.
items.reference: Treatment — Diet: "~3500 kcal = 1 lb of adipose tissue, an ~500-kcal restriction daily equates to weight reduction of 1 lb per week."

items.question: In a patient with metabolic syndrome WITHOUT diabetes who needs blood-pressure treatment, what is the preferred initial agent?
options: An ACE inhibitor or angiotensin receptor blocker
items.explanation: An ACE inhibitor or ARB is the preferred initial antihypertensive in metabolic syndrome without diabetes — effective, well tolerated, and acting on the upregulated renin-angiotensin system. Diuretics, calcium channel blockers, beta-blockers, and mineralocorticoid antagonists (e.g., finerenone) are add-ons.
items.reference: Treatment — Blood Pressure: "the best choice for the initial antihypertensive medication is an angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor or an angiotensin II receptor blocker."

items.question: Which thiazolidinedione has been associated with REDUCED ASCVD events in patients with insulin resistance/prediabetes/type 2 diabetes (albeit with weight gain, fractures, and heart failure)?
items.explanation: A meta-analysis found pioglitazone reduced ASCVD events in patients with insulin resistance, prediabetes, and type 2 diabetes, though with adverse effects including weight gain, bone fracture, and congestive heart failure/edema. TZDs also benefit MAFLD.
items.reference: Treatment — Insulin Resistance: "the TZD pioglitazone versus placebo was associated with reduction in ASCVD events in patients with insulin resistance (metabolic syndrome), prediabetes, and type 2 diabetes."

items.question: At what fasting triglyceride level do guidelines recommend treatment primarily to prevent pancreatitis?
items.explanation: Guidelines recommend treating fasting triglycerides >500 mg/dL to prevent more severe hypertriglyceridemia and pancreatitis. Although TG >150 is a defining component, TG-lowering has not been proven to independently reduce ASCVD.
items.reference: Treatment — Triglycerides: "fasting triglycerides >500 mg/dL should be treated to prevent more serious hypertriglyceridemia and pancreatitis."

items.question: Which omega-3 fatty acid strategy has been associated with reduced ASCVD events in trials (e.g., JELIS, REDUCE-IT)?
items.explanation: The two omega-3 trials associated with ASCVD risk reduction — JELIS and REDUCE-IT — used EPA only, whereas STRENGTH (EPA plus DHA) was terminated for futility. Omega-3s lower fasting triglycerides ~25–40%.
items.reference: Treatment — Triglycerides: "The two omega-3 randomized controlled trials associated with ASCVD risk reduction, JELIS and REDUCE-IT, used EPA only, whereas STRENGTH, which was terminated prematurely because of futility, used EPA plus DHA."

items.question: Which statement about raising HDL-C in metabolic syndrome is correct?
options: Niacin raises HDL-C and reliably reduces cardiovascular events
Niacin can raise HDL-C up to ~30%, but raising HDL-C with niacin has not reduced ASCVD events
Ezetimibe is the best HDL-raising drug
items.explanation: Niacin is the only reliable HDL-raiser (up to ~30%), but trials of niacin added to statins showed no reduction in ASCVD events from raising HDL-C. Low HDL-C is a risk marker, not a causal therapeutic target.
items.reference: Treatment — HDL: "there is no evidence that raising HDL cholesterol with nicotinic acid beneficially affects ASCVD events."

items.question: In a patient with impaired fasting glucose (prediabetes), which is most effective at preventing progression to type 2 diabetes?
options: Lifestyle intervention (weight loss, diet, activity), which is more effective than metformin
items.explanation: Lifestyle intervention (weight reduction, saturated-fat restriction, increased activity) reduces incident type 2 diabetes more than metformin, which also reduces incidence but to a lesser degree. Lifestyle is therefore first-line for diabetes prevention.
items.reference: Impaired Fasting Glucose: "a lifestyle intervention... has been shown to reduce the incidence of type 2 diabetes. Metformin also reduces the incidence... although the effect is less pronounced than that of lifestyle intervention."

items.question: Which adipokine is insulin-SENSITIZING and anti-inflammatory, and is typically REDUCED in metabolic syndrome?
items.explanation: Adiponectin, produced by adipocytes, enhances insulin sensitivity (suppresses hepatic gluconeogenesis, increases muscle glucose uptake/fatty-acid oxidation via AMP kinase) and is anti-inflammatory; its levels are reduced in metabolic syndrome. Resistin, TNF-α, and PAI-1 promote insulin resistance/inflammation.
items.reference: Adiponectin: "Adiponectin is an anti-inflammatory cytokine produced exclusively by adipocytes. Adiponectin enhances insulin sensitivity... Reductions in adiponectin levels are common in metabolic syndrome."

items.question: Which physical findings are most commonly elevated/present in metabolic syndrome?
items.explanation: Metabolic syndrome is typically asymptomatic; on exam, elevated waist circumference and blood pressure are the usual findings and should prompt a search for the biochemical components. Acanthosis nigricans/lipoatrophy occur less commonly (severe insulin resistance).
items.reference: Clinical Features: "On physical examination, waist circumference and blood pressure are often elevated."

items.question: Which weight-loss medication combination should be AVOIDED in a patient with a seizure disorder or uncontrolled hypertension?
items.explanation: Naltrexone/bupropion is contraindicated in seizure disorders (or conditions predisposing to seizures) and increases pulse and blood pressure, so it should not be used in uncontrolled hypertension. Phentermine/topiramate and GLP-1RAs have different cautions.
items.reference: Treatment — Obesity: "the drug combination is contraindicated in patients with seizure disorders or any condition that predisposes to seizures. Naltrexone/bupropion also increases pulse and blood pressure and should not be given to patients with uncontrolled hypertension."

items.question: Which agent produces the greatest average weight loss in the listed trials?
items.explanation: Tirzepatide (a dual GIP/GLP-1 receptor agonist) produced dose-dependent weight loss up to ~20.9%, exceeding semaglutide 2.4 mg (~15%), phentermine/topiramate (~8%), liraglutide 3.0 mg (~6%), and orlistat (~4%).
items.reference: Treatment — Obesity: "Participants experienced a dose-dependent reduction in weight ranging from -15.0% with 5 mg of tirzepatide weekly to 20.9% with the 15-mg dose."

items.question: Metabolic (bariatric) surgery is an option for metabolic syndrome at which BMI threshold?
options: BMI >40, or >35 with comorbidities (and increasingly as low as 30 with type 2 diabetes)
items.explanation: Metabolic surgery is indicated at BMI >40, or >35 with comorbidities, with an evolving application at BMI as low as 30 in patients with type 2 diabetes. Gastric bypass/sleeve gastrectomy improve most components and gastric bypass has shown a survival benefit.
items.reference: Treatment — Obesity: "Metabolic or bariatric surgery is an important option for patients with metabolic syndrome who have a BMI >40 kg/m² or >35 kg/m² with comorbidities. An evolving application... includes patients with a BMI as low as 30 kg/m² and type 2 diabetes."

items.question: In insulin resistance, how is insulin's effect on the vasculature and kidney altered to promote hypertension?
options: Both vasodilation and sodium reabsorption are lost
The vasodilatory (nitric-oxide) effect is lost while sodium reabsorption and sympathetic activation are preserved
items.explanation: In insulin resistance, insulin's nitric-oxide-mediated vasodilation is lost, but its renal sodium-reabsorbing effect and sympathetic activation persist — favoring sodium retention, increased sympathetic tone, and hypertension. Adipose angiotensinogen and hyperuricemia also contribute.
items.reference: Hypertension: "in the setting of insulin resistance, the vasodilatory effect of insulin is lost but the renal effect on sodium reabsorption is preserved. Insulin also increases the activity of the sympathetic nervous system."

items.question: In a premenopausal woman with metabolic syndrome features and anovulation/hyperandrogenism, which tests help confirm associated PCOS?
items.explanation: PCOS is highly associated with insulin resistance and metabolic syndrome; if suspected (anovulation, hyperandrogenism), measure testosterone, LH, and FSH. Metformin (an insulin sensitizer) benefits PCOS, underscoring the shared insulin-resistance mechanism.
items.reference: Diagnosis: "If polycystic ovary syndrome is suspected based on clinical features and anovulation, testosterone, luteinizing hormone, and follicle-stimulating hormone should be measured."

items.question: How does the prevalence of metabolic syndrome change with age in the U.S. population?
options: It increases with age, affecting nearly half of those over 60
It is constant across all ages
It only occurs in people under 40
items.explanation: Prevalence rises with age — from ~19.5% at 20–39 years to ~48.6% at ≥60 years (nearly half of those over 60), with women more often affected after 60. Childhood obesity is now extending the syndrome into younger ages.
items.reference: Aging: "The metabolic syndrome affects nearly 50% of the U.S. population aged >60, and at >60 years of age, women are more often affected."

items.question: How much daily physical activity is generally required to achieve weight loss in metabolic syndrome?
items.explanation: About 60–90 minutes of moderate-to-high-intensity activity daily is required to achieve weight loss, though even 30 minutes of moderate activity daily confers health benefit. Routine activities (walking, gardening) count toward the total.
items.reference: Treatment — Physical Activity: "60–90 min of moderate- to high-intensity daily activity is required to achieve this goal... a health benefit will follow from at least 30 min of moderate-intensity activity daily."

items.question: When a fibrate must be combined with a statin, which fibrate is generally preferred to reduce myopathy risk?
items.explanation: When combined with statins (many of which are metabolized by CYP3A4), fenofibrate is preferred over gemfibrozil because gemfibrozil more strongly increases the risk of statin-associated myopathy.
items.reference: Treatment — Triglycerides: "Concomitant administration with drugs metabolized by the 3A4 cytochrome P450 system (including some statins) increases the risk of myopathy. In these cases, fenofibrate may be preferable to gemfibrozil."

items.question: Compared with <1 hour/day of screen time, more than 4 hours/day is associated with what change in metabolic-syndrome risk?
items.explanation: More than 4 hours/day of television/video/computer use (vs <1 hour) is associated with a roughly twofold increased risk of metabolic syndrome, reflecting the contribution of sedentary behavior to the syndrome's components.
items.reference: Sedentary Lifestyle: "those who do so for >4 h daily have a twofold increased risk of the metabolic syndrome."

items.question: Why does insulin resistance tend to cause hyperuricemia?
options: Insulin increases uric acid production by the liver
Insulin enhances renal tubular reabsorption of uric acid
items.explanation: Insulin enhances renal tubular reabsorption of uric acid, so the hyperinsulinemic, insulin-resistant state raises serum urate. Uric acid may also contribute to hypertension by impairing macula-densa nitric oxide synthase and activating the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system.
items.reference: Hyperuricemia: "Hyperuricemia reflects defects in insulin action on the renal tubular reabsorption of uric acid."

items.statement: Being on drug treatment for elevated blood pressure or triglycerides can count toward meeting a metabolic-syndrome component.
items.explanation: True. The criteria explicitly allow specific medication for a component (e.g., antihypertensives, triglyceride-lowering drugs) to count as meeting that component.
items.reference: Table 420-1: components include thresholds 'or specific medication' (e.g., 'Hypertension: blood pressure ≥130 mmHg systolic or ≥85 mmHg diastolic or specific medication').

items.statement: Only obese individuals can have the metabolic syndrome.
items.explanation: False. Normal-weight individuals can be insulin-resistant and meet metabolic-syndrome criteria — a phenotype especially common in India, Southeast Asia, and Central America, reflecting relatively greater visceral adiposity.
items.reference: Risk Factors: "patients who are of normal weight may also be insulin-resistant and may have metabolic syndrome."

items.statement: Inhibition of lipolysis is the most insulin-sensitive action of insulin.
items.explanation: True. The anti-lipolytic effect is the most sensitive pathway of insulin action, so even mild insulin resistance disinhibits lipolysis and raises free fatty acids, perpetuating insulin resistance.
items.reference: Etiology: "the inhibition of lipolysis in adipose tissue is the most sensitive pathway of insulin action."

items.statement: A 5–10% weight reduction can improve insulin sensitivity and multiple components of the metabolic syndrome.
items.explanation: True. As little as 5% (and more so 10%) weight loss improves insulin sensitivity and favorably modifies many components (TG, HDL, glucose, BP), making weight loss the primary treatment.
items.reference: Treatment — Lifestyle: "With at least 5% and more so with 10% weight reduction, improvement in insulin sensitivity results in favorable modifications in many components."

items.statement: Raising HDL-C with niacin has been proven to reduce cardiovascular events in patients on statins.
items.explanation: False. Although niacin reliably raises HDL-C (up to ~30%), trials of niacin added to statins showed no reduction in ASCVD events from raising HDL-C; low HDL-C is a marker, not a causal target.
items.reference: Treatment — HDL: "there is no evidence that raising HDL cholesterol with nicotinic acid beneficially affects ASCVD events."

items.statement: CPAP treatment improves insulin sensitivity in patients with obstructive sleep apnea.
items.explanation: True. CPAP treatment improves insulin sensitivity in OSA, which is a modifiable contributor to insulin resistance — and OSA may predict metabolic syndrome even without obesity.
items.reference: Obstructive Sleep Apnea: "Continuous positive airway pressure treatment improves insulin sensitivity in patients with obstructive sleep apnea."

items.statement: Pharmacologic triglyceride lowering has been clearly proven to independently reduce cardiovascular outcomes in metabolic syndrome.
items.explanation: False. Lowering triglycerides with available agents has not been proven to be an independent predictor of cardiovascular outcomes; TG >500 is treated mainly to prevent pancreatitis.
items.reference: Treatment — Triglycerides: "lowering triglycerides with any of the pharmaceuticals has not been proven to be an independent predictor of CVD outcomes."

items.statement: Thiazolidinediones improve insulin-mediated glucose uptake in muscle and adipose tissue, whereas metformin does not.
items.explanation: True. Both metformin and TZDs enhance hepatic insulin action and suppress glucose production, but TZDs (not metformin) also improve insulin-mediated glucose uptake in muscle and adipose tissue.
items.reference: Treatment — Insulin Resistance: "TZDs, but not metformin, also improve insulin-mediated glucose uptake in muscle and adipose tissue."

items.statement: MAFLD is unrelated to cardiovascular disease.
items.explanation: False. MAFLD is independently related to cardiovascular disease, especially coronary artery disease, in addition to its risk of progression to MASH, cirrhosis, and hepatocellular carcinoma.
items.reference: MAFLD: "studies have shown that MAFLD is related independently to CVD, especially coronary artery disease."

items.statement: Most patients with type 2 diabetes or impaired glucose tolerance have the metabolic syndrome.
items.explanation: True. Approximately 75% of patients with type 2 diabetes or impaired glucose tolerance have metabolic syndrome, and its presence in these patients is associated with higher CVD prevalence.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus: "The great majority (~75%) of patients with type 2 diabetes or impaired glucose tolerance have metabolic syndrome."

items.statement: Metformin prevents type 2 diabetes more effectively than lifestyle intervention in people with impaired fasting glucose.
items.explanation: False — it is the reverse. Lifestyle intervention reduces incident type 2 diabetes more than metformin; metformin is effective but less so than lifestyle change.
items.reference: Impaired Fasting Glucose: "Metformin also reduces the incidence of diabetes, although the effect is less pronounced than that of lifestyle intervention."

items.statement: Adiponectin levels are typically increased in the metabolic syndrome.
items.explanation: False. Adiponectin (an insulin-sensitizing, anti-inflammatory adipokine) is typically REDUCED in metabolic syndrome, whereas proinflammatory adipokines (resistin, TNF-α, PAI-1) are increased.
items.reference: Adiponectin: "Reductions in adiponectin levels are common in metabolic syndrome."

items.statement: Orlistat reduces the incidence of type 2 diabetes, particularly in patients with impaired glucose tolerance.
items.explanation: True. Orlistat (which inhibits ~30% of fat absorption) reduced the incidence of type 2 diabetes, an effect especially evident among patients with impaired glucose tolerance at baseline.
items.reference: Treatment — Obesity: "orlistat reduced the incidence of type 2 diabetes, an effect that was especially evident among patients with impaired glucose tolerance at baseline."

items.statement: Waist circumference alone reliably distinguishes subcutaneous from visceral abdominal fat.
items.explanation: False. Waist circumference does not reliably distinguish subcutaneous from visceral fat; that distinction requires imaging (DEXA, CT, or MRI). This is why the same waist confers different risk across populations.
items.reference: Increased Waist Circumference: "measuring waist circumference does not reliably distinguish increases in SC abdominal adipose tissue from that in intra-abdominal or visceral fat; this distinction requires dual X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA), computed tomography (CT), or magnetic resonance imaging (MRI)."

items.assertion: Excess free fatty acids contribute to insulin resistance in metabolic syndrome.
items.reason: Because anti-lipolysis is the most insulin-sensitive action of insulin, even mild insulin resistance increases lipolysis and free fatty acid release.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Since suppression of lipolysis is the most sensitive insulin action, insulin resistance disinhibits lipolysis early, raising free fatty acids that further impair insulin action — a self-reinforcing cycle.
items.reference: Etiology: "the inhibition of lipolysis in adipose tissue is the most sensitive pathway of insulin action. Thus, when insulin resistance develops, increased lipolysis produces more fatty acids."

items.assertion: Visceral fat has a greater effect on hepatic metabolism than subcutaneous fat.
items.reason: Visceral adipose tissue drains into the portal circulation, delivering free fatty acids directly to the liver.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Portal drainage exposes the liver to high free-fatty-acid concentrations from visceral fat, increasing hepatic glucose/VLDL production, whereas subcutaneous fat drains systemically.
items.reference: Increased Waist Circumference: "With increases in visceral adipose tissue, adipose tissue-derived free fatty acids reach the liver more readily."

items.assertion: Weight reduction is the primary treatment for metabolic syndrome.
items.reason: Central obesity is the driving force, and weight loss improves insulin sensitivity and multiple components simultaneously.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because central adiposity drives the insulin resistance underlying the syndrome, even 5–10% weight loss improves insulin sensitivity and several components at once.
items.reference: Treatment — Lifestyle: "Obesity, particularly abdominal, is the driving force behind metabolic syndrome. Thus, weight reduction is the primary approach."

items.reason: In insulin resistance, insulin's vasodilatory effect is lost while its sodium-retaining and sympathetic-activating effects persist.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Loss of nitric-oxide-mediated vasodilation with preserved sodium reabsorption and sympathetic activation tilts the balance toward hypertension.
items.reference: Hypertension: "in the setting of insulin resistance, the vasodilatory effect of insulin is lost but the renal effect on sodium reabsorption is preserved."

items.assertion: Severe hypertriglyceridemia (>500 mg/dL) in metabolic syndrome is treated pharmacologically.
items.reason: Severe hypertriglyceridemia carries a risk of acute pancreatitis.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. The principal indication to treat TG >500 is to prevent pancreatitis; TG-lowering has not been proven to independently reduce ASCVD.
items.reference: Treatment — Triglycerides: "fasting triglycerides >500 mg/dL should be treated to prevent more serious hypertriglyceridemia and pancreatitis."

items.assertion: Metabolic syndrome predisposes to fatty liver disease.
items.reason: Insulin resistance increases free-fatty-acid flux to the liver and reduces intrahepatic fatty-acid oxidation, raising triglyceride accumulation.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. The insulin-resistant state delivers more fatty acids to the liver and lowers their oxidation, driving hepatic triglyceride accumulation (MAFLD) that can progress to MASH.
items.reference: MAFLD: "The mechanism relates to increases in free fatty acid flux and reductions in intrahepatic fatty acid oxidation with resultant increases in triglyceride biosynthesis and hepatocellular accumulation."

items.assertion: Lifestyle intervention is preferred over metformin for preventing diabetes in prediabetes.
items.reason: Lifestyle intervention reduces the incidence of type 2 diabetes more than metformin does.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because lifestyle change reduces incident diabetes more effectively than metformin, it is first-line for diabetes prevention in impaired fasting glucose.
items.reference: Impaired Fasting Glucose: "a lifestyle intervention... has been shown to reduce the incidence of type 2 diabetes. Metformin also reduces the incidence... although the effect is less pronounced."

items.assertion: All patients with metabolic syndrome should be screened for obstructive sleep apnea.
items.reason: OSA is associated with insulin resistance and may predict metabolic syndrome even without excess adiposity.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because OSA contributes to insulin resistance and can predict the syndrome independent of obesity, screening (and treating with CPAP, which improves insulin sensitivity) is recommended.
items.reference: Diagnosis/OSA: "The medical history should include evaluation of symptoms for obstructive sleep apnea in all patients... obstructive sleep apnea may predict metabolic syndrome, even in the absence of excess adiposity."

items.assertion: Reduced adiponectin contributes to insulin resistance in metabolic syndrome.
items.reason: Adiponectin enhances insulin sensitivity by suppressing hepatic glucose production and increasing muscle glucose uptake and fatty-acid oxidation.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Since adiponectin is insulin-sensitizing, its reduction in metabolic syndrome removes a protective influence, contributing to insulin resistance.
items.reference: Adiponectin: "Adiponectin enhances insulin sensitivity... In the liver, adiponectin inhibits... glucose production. In muscle, adiponectin increases glucose transport and enhances fatty acid oxidation... Reductions in adiponectin levels are common in metabolic syndrome."

items.assertion: Fenofibrate is preferred over gemfibrozil when a fibrate is combined with a statin.
items.reason: Gemfibrozil increases the risk of statin-associated myopathy more than fenofibrate.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because gemfibrozil interferes with statin metabolism and raises myopathy risk more than fenofibrate, fenofibrate is the preferred fibrate for statin combinations.
items.reference: Treatment — Triglycerides: "Concomitant administration with drugs metabolized by the 3A4 cytochrome P450 system (including some statins) increases the risk of myopathy. In these cases, fenofibrate may be preferable to gemfibrozil."

items.assertion: Lipodystrophy is associated with severe metabolic syndrome despite a lack of generalized obesity.
items.reason: Loss of functional adipose storage forces lipid into ectopic sites, producing severe insulin resistance.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because lipodystrophy impairs healthy fat storage, lipids accumulate ectopically (liver/muscle), causing severe insulin resistance and the metabolic-syndrome components even without overall obesity.
items.reference: Lipodystrophy: "Both genetic lipodystrophy... and acquired lipodystrophy... may give rise to severe insulin resistance and many of the components of metabolic syndrome."

items.assertion: Insulin resistance is associated with hyperuricemia.
items.reason: Insulin enhances renal tubular reabsorption of uric acid.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because insulin promotes renal uric-acid reabsorption, the hyperinsulinemic insulin-resistant state raises serum urate; uric acid may in turn contribute to hypertension.
items.reference: Hyperuricemia: "Hyperuricemia reflects defects in insulin action on the renal tubular reabsorption of uric acid."
